title: The connection between urinary urgency and anxiety activation
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

 You're standing there for the third time in an hour. Your bladder feels full, urgent, insistent. But when you sit down...
metaDescription:
The bathroom door clicks shut. Again. 

 You're standing there for the third time in an hour. Your bladder feels full, urgent, insistent. But when you sit...
htmlContent:
The bathroom door clicks shut. Again.

You're standing there for the third time in an hour. Your bladder feels full, urgent, insistent. But when you sit down, barely anything comes out. Or maybe a lot comes out, and you think "good, that's done" but twenty minutes later the signal fires again. Full. Urgent. Now.

I know this loop. I've lived this loop. And for years, I thought it was a bladder problem. A urology problem. Something wrong with my pelvic floor or my prostate or my hydration habits. I did the elimination diets. I did the kegels. I did the "just hold it longer" training. And none of it fixed the core issue, because the core issue wasn't in my bladder at all.

Here's the thing. Your bladder is wired directly into your nervous system. And your nervous system, when it's been shaped by trauma, doesn't know how to turn off the alarm. So it keeps sending urgency signals to your bladder. Not because your bladder is full. But because your brain thinks you're in danger.

Stay with me here. This connection between urinary urgency and anxiety activation is real, it's physiological, and it's something almost no one talks about in the trauma recovery space. Let's look at what's actually happening under the hood.

The pelvic nervous system is not a plumbing system

Bessel van der Kolk, in his research at the Trauma Center in Boston, documented something that should change how we think about chronic urinary issues. He found that people with trauma histories show altered activation patterns in the periaqueductal gray, a midbrain structure that coordinates both threat responses and visceral sensations including bladder fullness.

Think about that. The same part of your brain that decides "we're under attack" is also the part that decides "your bladder is full." They share circuitry. They share bandwidth. When one line lights up, the other one flickers too.

I remember sitting in a urologist's office, describing my symptoms. Frequent urination. Sudden urgency. Waking up three or four times a night. He ran the standard tests. Ultrasound. Flow study. Post-void residual. Everything came back normal. "You have an overactive bladder," he said, and handed me a prescription for an anticholinergic medication.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

The medication helped a little. It dulled the sensation. But it didn't stop the underlying pattern, because the pattern wasn't about bladder muscle spasms. It was about my nervous system being stuck in a state of high alert. And high alert means your body prepares for action. It diverts resources away from digestion, away from rest, and yes, away from normal bladder function.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body's "felt sense." Your bladder has a felt sense. When you're anxious, that felt sense changes. The muscles around your bladder tighten. The signal from your bladder to your brain gets amplified. What was a mild sensation of fullness becomes an emergency.

It's not in your head. It's in your nervous system. And your nervous system is in your whole body, including your bladder.

The vagus nerve and the bladder's false alarms

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, mapped out how the vagus nerve connects your brain to your internal organs. The vagus nerve runs from your brainstem down through your chest and abdomen, touching your heart, your lungs, your digestive tract, and yes, your bladder. It's the body's main information superhighway for safety and danger signals.

When your vagus nerve detects safety, it sends signals that allow your body to rest, digest, and hold urine normally. When your vagus nerve detects danger, it shifts into a different mode. It activates the sympathetic nervous system, the fight or flight branch. And in fight or flight mode, your bladder gets the message to empty. Quickly. Because if you're about to run from a predator, you don't want a full bladder slowing you down.

Here's where it gets tricky. Your vagus nerve doesn't only respond to real threats. It responds to perceived threats. To internal threats. To memories of threats. To the feeling of a tight chest or a racing heart that reminds your body of past danger. So you're sitting at your desk, not being chased by anything, but your nervous system says "danger" and your bladder says "empty now."

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana, who wrote extensively on applying Porges' work in clinical settings, describes how tracking your nervous system state can help you recognize when urgency signals are coming from threat activation rather than actual bladder fullness. It's a shift in how you interpret the signal. "Oh, this isn't my bladder. This is my nervous system saying I'm not safe."

I started noticing patterns. My urgency would spike during meetings with certain people. Right before I had to give a presentation. Late at night when I was alone with my thoughts. These weren't random. They were linked to specific triggers that my nervous system had learned to associate with danger.

The false alarms kept coming until I started addressing the underlying activation. Not by fighting the urgency, but by calming the system that was generating it.

Why the standard advice makes it worse

Here's what I was told by well-meaning doctors and physical therapists. "Drink less water before bed." "Avoid caffeine and alcohol." "Do pelvic floor exercises." "Use the bathroom on a schedule, not just when you feel the urge."

All of this advice assumes the problem is mechanical. That your bladder is simply too sensitive or your pelvic floor is too weak. And for some people, that's part of the picture. But for those of us with trauma histories, the mechanical approach misses the point entirely.

When you try to "hold it longer" as bladder training suggests, you're asking your nervous system to override a safety signal. Your brain is saying "danger, empty the bladder" and you're saying "no, sit with the discomfort." For a traumatized nervous system, that feels like betrayal. It feels like ignoring a fire alarm because the fire is small. But the alarm keeps ringing because the system hasn't learned that there's no fire at all.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work in this area focuses on building ventral vagal safety, the state where your nervous system feels grounded and connected. When you're in ventral vagal, your bladder signals normalize. You feel fullness, not emergency. You can wait, not because you're forcing yourself to, but because the threat response isn't overriding everything else.

Let me ask you something. When was the last time you felt truly relaxed in your body? Not distracted. Not numbed out. Actually relaxed. If you can't remember, that tells you something important. Your nervous system may have forgotten what rest feels like. It may have decided, somewhere along the way, that rest is dangerous.

Deb Dana talks about this - how the dorsal vagal state can look like relaxation from the outside but actually feels like collapse on the inside. There's a difference between choosing to rest and being unable to move. There's a difference between peace and shutdown. Your body knows the difference, even if the people around you can't tell.

Notice your hands right now. Are they clenched? Open? Cold? Warm? Your hands tell a story about your autonomic state. Cold hands often mean sympathetic activation - blood has moved to the core, preparing for threat. Warm, relaxed hands suggest ventral vagal engagement - safety. It's a small thing to notice. But small things are where healing lives.

I stopped the bladder training. I stopped the scheduled voiding. I started paying attention to what my body was actually saying. And what I found was that my urgency was almost always preceded by something else. A shallow breath. A tight jaw. A feeling of dread that I hadn't consciously noticed.

The urgency was the canary in the coal mine. It was the first signal that my nervous system was slipping into activation. And when I learned to catch the earlier signals, the urgency started to fade.

Releasing the pattern through the body

Janina Fisher, who integrates trauma treatment with sensorimotor approaches, talks about how trauma patterns get stored in the body's tissues, including the smooth muscle of the bladder and pelvic floor. These patterns aren't just psychological. They're physical. They're held in the fascia, in the muscle fibers, in the way your body organizes itself around a memory of threat.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Working with the hips, lower back, and thighs can help release tension that radiates into the pelvic floor. I'm not saying roll out your bladder directly. But the muscles around your bladder, the ones that tighten when you're anxious, they respond to gentle pressure and movement.

I started doing something simple. When I felt the urgency, instead of running to the bathroom, I would pause. I would put my hand on my lower belly. I would take three slow breaths. And I would ask myself: "Is this danger, or is this memory?"

Most of the time, it was memory. It was the ghost of some old threat that my body hadn't finished processing. The urgency was a residue. A fossil of a survival response that had outlived its usefulness.

I'm not saying you should never go to the bathroom. If you need to go, go. But notice what happens when you go. Does the relief last? Or does the urgency return quickly? If it returns quickly, you're not dealing with a full bladder. You're dealing with a nervous system that hasn't learned it's safe yet.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). This might seem unrelated, but hear me out. Noise sensitivity is often a sign of a hypervigilant nervous system. And a hypervigilant nervous system is a bladder-activating nervous system. Reducing sensory input can help your vagus nerve shift toward safety, which in turn calms the urgency signals.

I wear them sometimes when I'm working in a coffee shop or trying to sleep. The reduction in background noise helps my nervous system settle. And when my nervous system settles, my bladder settles too.

The open ending

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I still have days where the urgency comes back. Days where my bladder feels like it's running the show. But I understand now what's happening. I know that the urgency is a message, not a malfunction. It's my body telling me that something in my environment or my internal state feels unsafe.

I don't fight it anymore. I don't try to override it with willpower or bladder training. I acknowledge it. I breathe into it. I ask my nervous system what it needs to feel safe. Sometimes it needs a slower pace. Sometimes it needs to be heard. Sometimes it just needs me to stop treating my bladder like an enemy and start treating it like a messenger.

Your urinary urgency might not be about your bladder at all. It might be about your history. Your survival patterns. Your nervous system's best attempt to keep you safe, even when the danger is long gone.

Sit with that. See if it fits. And if it does, maybe the path forward isn't about fixing your bladder. Maybe it's about letting your nervous system know, slowly and repeatedly, that you're safe now.
